title: Neuroscience Fundamentals
description: Introduction to the structure and function of the nervous system, spanning from cellular neurobiology (action potentials, synapses) to systems (sensory processing, learning, and memory).

modules.title: Cellular & Molecular Foundations
modules.objective: Understand the basic building blocks of the nervous system, including the structure of neurons and how electrical and chemical signals are generated and transmitted.

topics.title: Anatomy of the Nervous System
concepts: Neurons: The primary signaling cells of the nervous system (soma, dendrites, axon, axon terminal).
Glia: Support cells including astrocytes (blood-brain barrier), oligodendrocytes (CNS myelin), and Schwann cells (PNS myelin).
Central Nervous System (CNS) vs. Peripheral Nervous System (PNS).
Gray matter (cell bodies) vs. White matter (myelinated axons).
key_facts: Neurons are specialized for the reception, conduction, and transmission of electrochemical signals.
Myelin sheaths created by glial cells dramatically increase the speed of action potential conduction.
The CNS consists of the brain and spinal cord, while the PNS connects the CNS to the rest of the body.
drills: Draw a neuron and label the soma, dendrites, axon, myelin sheath, and axon terminal.
Compare and contrast the functions of oligodendrocytes and Schwann cells.
common_mistakes: Thinking neurons are the only important cells in the brain; glia are critical for support and signaling.
Confusing the CNS and PNS divisions.

topics.title: Bioelectricity & Action Potentials
concepts: Resting Membrane Potential: Usually around -70mV, maintained by the Na+/K+ pump and leak channels.
Action Potential: A rapid, all-or-nothing change in membrane potential used for long-distance signaling.
Depolarization (Na+ rushing in) and Repolarization (K+ rushing out).
Saltatory Conduction: The jumping of action potentials from node to node in myelinated axons.
key_facts: Action potentials are triggered when the membrane depolarization reaches a specific threshold (e.g., -55mV).
The Na+/K+ pump uses ATP to actively transport 3 Na+ ions out for every 2 K+ ions in, maintaining the concentration gradients.
Action potentials do not degrade in strength as they travel down the axon.
drills: Graph an action potential over time, labeling the resting state, threshold, depolarization, repolarization, and hyperpolarization phases.
Explain what would happen to an action potential if voltage-gated sodium channels were blocked by a toxin (e.g., TTX).
common_mistakes: Believing an action potential can be 'stronger' or 'weaker' (it's all-or-nothing; stimulus intensity is coded by frequency).
Confusing the direction of ion flow during depolarization vs. repolarization.

topics.title: Synaptic Transmission
concepts: Synapse: The junction between two neurons.
Chemical Synapses: Use neurotransmitters released from vesicles into the synaptic cleft.
Electrical Synapses: Direct cytoplasmic connections via gap junctions (faster, less modifiable).
Excitatory (EPSP) vs. Inhibitory (IPSP) postsynaptic potentials.
key_facts: Calcium (Ca2+) influx at the axon terminal is the critical trigger for neurotransmitter vesicle fusion and release.
Neurotransmitters bind to specific receptors (ionotropic or metabotropic) on the postsynaptic membrane.
A single neuron integrates thousands of EPSPs and IPSPs to determine if it will fire an action potential.
drills: Sequence the steps of chemical synaptic transmission from action potential arrival to neurotransmitter clearance.
Differentiate between spatial summation and temporal summation of synaptic inputs.
common_mistakes: Thinking neurotransmitters enter the postsynaptic cell (they usually only bind to receptors on the surface).

modules.title: Sensory & Motor Systems
modules.objective: Explore how the nervous system detects environmental stimuli and generates coordinated physical responses.

topics.title: Sensory Transduction & Processing
concepts: Sensory Transduction: The process of converting physical stimuli (light, sound waves, pressure) into electrical neural signals.
Photoreceptors (rods and cones) in the retina for vision.
Hair cells in the cochlea for audition (hearing).
Topographic mapping: How sensory surfaces are mapped onto the cortex (e.g., retinotopic map, somatosensory homunculus).
key_facts: Rods are highly sensitive to light (night vision), while cones process color and detail (daylight vision).
The thalamus acts as the major sensory relay station for all senses except olfaction (smell).
Sensory pathways often cross over (decussate) to the opposite side of the brain.
drills: Trace the visual pathway from the retina to the primary visual cortex (V1).
Explain how the basilar membrane in the cochlea distinguishes between high and low-frequency sounds.
common_mistakes: Thinking the image on the retina is exactly what the brain 'sees' (the brain actively constructs visual perception).
Forgetting that smell bypasses the thalamus.

topics.title: Motor Control
concepts: Upper Motor Neurons (in the brain) vs. Lower Motor Neurons (in the spinal cord).
The Neuromuscular Junction (NMJ): The synapse between a motor neuron and a muscle fiber using acetylcholine (ACh).
Basal Ganglia: Involved in the initiation and smooth execution of voluntary movements.
Cerebellum: Responsible for motor learning, coordination, and balance.
key_facts: Damage to the basal ganglia leads to movement disorders like Parkinson's disease (hypokinesia) or Huntington's disease (hyperkinesia).
A motor unit consists of a single lower motor neuron and all the muscle fibers it innervates.
Reflex arcs can operate entirely within the spinal cord, without requiring brain input.
drills: Map the corticospinal tract from the primary motor cortex to a muscle in the hand.
Compare the roles of the basal ganglia and the cerebellum in motor control.
common_mistakes: Confusing the roles of the upper and lower motor neurons.
Thinking all movement is consciously controlled by the cortex (ignoring spinal reflexes and cerebellar automation).

modules.title: Systems & Behavioral Neuroscience
modules.objective: Understand the neural circuits underlying complex behaviors, including basic survival drives, emotion, and memory formation.

topics.title: Homeostasis & Motivation
concepts: Homeostasis: Maintaining internal biological stability (temperature, energy levels, hydration).
Hypothalamus: The master regulator of homeostasis and the endocrine system.
Circadian Rhythms: The ~24-hour biological clock driven by the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN).
Dopaminergic reward pathway: The Ventral Tegmental Area (VTA) and Nucleus Accumbens involved in motivation and addiction.
key_facts: The hypothalamus controls the pituitary gland, bridging the nervous and endocrine (hormone) systems.
Light suppresses melatonin production via a pathway from the retina to the SCN to the pineal gland.
Drugs of abuse hijack the brain's natural reward system by artificially spiking dopamine levels.
drills: Diagram the feedback loop that regulates blood osmolarity (thirst) involving the hypothalamus.
Explain how blue light exposure before bed disrupts sleep cycles.
common_mistakes: Viewing dopamine strictly as a 'pleasure' chemical rather than a signal of 'reward prediction error' and motivation.
Confusing the hypothalamus (homeostasis) with the hippocampus (memory).

topics.title: Emotion, Learning & Memory
concepts: Limbic System: A network of structures (including amygdala and hippocampus) involved in emotion and memory.
Amygdala: Critical for processing emotions, particularly fear, and emotional associative learning.
Hippocampus: Essential for the consolidation of short-term memory into long-term declarative memory.
Long-Term Potentiation (LTP): The cellular basis of learning; 'cells that fire together, wire together'.
key_facts: Patient H.M., who had his bilateral hippocampus removed, suffered from profound anterograde amnesia (inability to form new declarative memories).
Memory is not stored in a single 'box' but distributed across the cortex.
LTP involves the strengthening of synaptic connections, often mediated by NMDA and AMPA glutamate receptors.
drills: Describe the molecular mechanisms of LTP involving NMDA and AMPA receptors.
Differentiate between declarative (explicit) memory and procedural (implicit) memory, noting which brain structures are involved.
common_mistakes: Thinking memories are perfectly recorded like a video camera (they are reconstructive and prone to error).
Believing the hippocampus is where long-term memories are permanently stored.
